title: GPIO Functions on the Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040)

zones.label: Digital Output
zones.summary: Drive pins HIGH (3.3 V) or LOW (0 V) — all 26 GPIO
facts: Available on all 26 GPIO pins (GP0–GP28)
MicroPython: Pin(n, Pin.OUT)
3.3 V logic; can toggle up to ~80 MHz via PIO (Programmable I/O)
Typical uses: LEDs, relays, H-bridge direction signals, transistor switches

zones.label: Digital Input
zones.summary: Read HIGH or LOW from external signals — all 26 GPIO
facts: Available on all 26 GPIO pins (GP0–GP28)
MicroPython: Pin(n, Pin.IN, Pin.PULL_UP)
Built-in 50 kΩ pull-up or pull-down resistors (software configurable)
Typical uses: buttons, switches, encoder signals, digital sensors

zones.label: PWM
zones.summary: Pulse Width Modulation — analog-like output on any GPIO
facts: Available on all 26 GPIO pins via 8 PWM slices × 2 channels = 16 unique channels
MicroPython: PWM(Pin(n))
Frequency range: 8 Hz to 62.5 MHz
Duty cycle: 16-bit resolution (0–65,535) for smooth control
Typical uses: LED brightness dimming, servo position, motor speed, buzzer tones

zones.label: ADC
facts: Only 3 external ADC-capable pins: GP26, GP27, GP28
Plus an internal temperature sensor on ADC4 (not a physical pin)
MicroPython: ADC(Pin(26)) — read() returns 0–65,535
12-bit SAR ADC; input range 0–3.3 V; sample rate ~500 kSps
Typical uses: potentiometers, LDR light sensors, microphone, thermistor

zones.label: I2C
zones.summary: 2-wire serial bus — any GPIO, hardware blocks I2C0 and I2C1
facts: Can be mapped to any 2 GPIO pins; two hardware I2C blocks (I2C0, I2C1)
MicroPython: I2C(0, scl=Pin(1), sda=Pin(0), freq=400_000)
Standard 100 kbps · Fast 400 kbps · Fast+ 1 Mbps
Typical devices: OLED displays (SSD1306), BMP280, MPU-6050, RTC modules

zones.label: SPI
zones.summary: 4-wire full-duplex bus — any GPIO, hardware blocks SPI0 and SPI1
facts: Can be mapped to any 4 GPIO pins; two hardware SPI blocks (SPI0, SPI1)
MicroPython: SPI(0, baudrate=1_000_000, sck=Pin(6), mosi=Pin(7), miso=Pin(4))
Up to 62.5 MHz (full-duplex)
Typical devices: SD cards, TFT color displays (ILI9341), ADC chips, NOR flash

zones.label: UART
zones.summary: Async serial link — any GPIO, hardware blocks UART0 and UART1
facts: Can be mapped to any 2 GPIO pins; two hardware UART blocks (UART0, UART1)
MicroPython: UART(0, baudrate=9600, tx=Pin(0), rx=Pin(1))
Up to 921,600 baud (configurable)
Typical devices: GPS modules, HC-05 Bluetooth, serial debug REPL console

quiz.question: Which GPIO function is limited to only 3 external pins on the Pico (GP26, GP27, GP28)?
quiz.explanation: The RP2040 has only 3 ADC-capable GPIO pins (GP26, GP27, GP28) plus one internal channel for the temperature sensor. All other functions are available on any of the 26 GPIO pins.

quiz.question: Which function provides a 16-bit resolution output for smooth control of LEDs, servos, and motors?
quiz.explanation: PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) gives 16-bit duty-cycle control (0–65,535 steps) on any GPIO pin, which is fine enough for smooth LED dimming and precise servo positioning.

quiz.question: Which GPIO function lets you read the voltage level of a potentiometer or light sensor?
quiz.explanation: ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) converts a continuous 0–3.3 V input to a 12-bit number. Potentiometers, LDRs, and microphones all produce analog voltages that need an ADC pin.

quiz.question: Which function is used to turn an LED on, control a relay, or set the direction of an H-bridge?
quiz.explanation: Digital Output drives a pin to 3.3 V (HIGH) or 0 V (LOW), making it perfect for LEDs, relays, and H-bridge direction signals. Use Pin(n, Pin.OUT) in MicroPython.

quiz.question: Which GPIO function lets you connect to OLED displays and sensors like the BMP280 on a shared 2-wire bus?
quiz.explanation: I2C uses just 2 wires (SCL and SDA) and allows up to 127 devices on the same bus. The SSD1306 OLED and BMP280 sensor both use I2C with unique 7-bit addresses.
